abstract: From 1951 to 1991, approximately 17 distinct processes were developed to recover uranium from wet phosphoric acid (WPA); however, the industrial viability of these methods has historically fluctuated alongside volatile market prices. Today, the extraction of uranium from WPA has regained significant interest, driven by rising global demand and the rapid expansion of nuclear energy programs in developing nations. This renewed attention serves as a catalyst for both refining legacy technologies and exploring entirely new methodological approaches. This paper provides a comprehensive review of historical extraction processes and the underlying physicochemistry that governs them. Furthermore, we examine and discuss recent advancements in the development of novel extraction systems aimed at improving efficiency and sustainability in uranium recovery.
